question: State and briefly explain the Church-Turing thesis. Why is it a thesis and not a theorem?
expectedPoints: The Church-Turing thesis states that any function that is effectively computable is computable by a Turing machine.
It is a thesis rather than a theorem because it is a fundamental statement about the nature of computation that cannot be formally proven.
It serves as a foundational principle that defines the boundaries of what can be computed by any mechanical process.

question: Explain the significance of the Halting Problem. Describe the key steps in the proof of its undecidability and its implications for the theory of computation.
expectedPoints: The Halting Problem is the problem of determining whether a given program will halt or run forever on a specific input.
It is proven undecidable by a diagonalization argument, assuming a decider exists and then constructing a contradiction.
The proof shows that no such general algorithm can exist for all possible program-input pairs.
This was one of the first problems proven undecidable and establishes a fundamental limit on what can be computed.
It implies the existence of other non-computable problems and is foundational to the classification of decidability and tractability.

question: Compare and contrast the classes P, NP, and NP-complete. Explain the significance of the P vs NP problem, including what it would mean if P were equal to NP. Discuss at least one known NP-complete problem and the technique used to prove its completeness.
expectedPoints: P is the class of problems solvable in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine.
NP is the class of problems verifiable in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine.
NP-complete are the hardest problems in NP; if any NP-complete problem is in P, then P=NP.
The P vs NP problem asks whether every problem that can be verified quickly can also be solved quickly.
If P=NP, it would have profound implications for cryptography, optimization, and artificial intelligence, as many currently intractable problems would become efficiently solvable.
The Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) is a canonical NP-complete problem.
Its NP-completeness was proven by Cook-Levin theorem, which demonstrated that SAT is in NP and that every problem in NP can be reduced to it in polynomial time.
